analysis:
- Common positive structure: the stronger heuristics (TPOSS, GSI_LS, the top hybrid) combine a candidate-reduction phase (Pareto filtering or box-volume proxy), a greedy constructive phase (exact or lazy greedy/CELF), and a small bounded local search (swap improvements). This pattern balances quality vs HV-call cost.
- TPOSS (Heuristic 1) explores with evolutionary targeted flips and an environment-selection archive: good at exploring sizes and escaping local optima but pays many HV calls and array copies → higher cost and variable runtime.
- GSI_LS (Heuristic 2) deterministically keeps set size via insert+remove and then exhaustive pairwise swaps; it's simple and robust but recomputes hypervolume aggressively (O(n·k) HVs) and scales poorly.
- The best hybrid (Heuristic 3) wins by engineering: fast nondominated filtering, cheap box-volume proxy, small capped shortlist, CELF-style lazy greedy with timestamps (avoids recomputation), explicit caps on HV evaluations, and a tiny swap search — minimal HV calls for high-quality picks.
- Repeated weaknesses across lower-ranked heuristics: insufficient caching of marginal gains, no lazy evaluation (causing redundant HV computations), large unbounded candidate pools, naive nondominated filters (O(n^2) loops without efficient pruning), frequent creation of temporaries and stacking arrays, inconsistent handling of edge cases (k>n), and unbounded local search budgets.
- Data-reuse opportunities frequently missed: maintain current HV and reuse it when computing gains; cache (candidate, selection_size) → marginal_gain; compute distances/vector norms in batch; avoid rebuilding arrays inside inner loops.
- Efficiency levers that matter most: (1) strong candidate pruning (Pareto + small proxy shortlist), (2) lazy greedy/CELF with timestamped cached gains, (3) tight caps on HV calls and shortlist sizes (e.g., O(max(k, constant))), (4) bounded deterministic swap refinement, and (5) parallelizing independent HV evaluations when available.
- Numerical/robustness notes: add small eps to box-volume proxies, validate reference-point dimensionality, and avoid accepting many tiny gains due to numerical noise.
experience: Favor a Pareto filter, form a tiny proxy-ranked shortlist (O(max(k,200))), run CELF-style lazy greedy with cached marginal gains and strict HV-call caps, then apply a bounded first‑improvement swap search. Use vectorized distance/proxy computations, efficient nondominated sorting, and deterministic caps/seeding; parallelize independent HV evaluations. This yields high hypervolume with predictable, low runtime.
current_reflection: Favor a Pareto filter, form a tiny proxy-ranked shortlist (O(max(k,200))), run CELF-style lazy greedy with cached marginal gains and strict HV-call caps, then apply a bounded first‑improvement swap search. Use vectorized distance/proxy computations, efficient nondominated sorting, and deterministic caps/seeding; parallelize independent HV evaluations. This yields high hypervolume with predictable, low runtime.
good_reflections: Prefer a hybrid constructive: filter dominated points, approximate contributions with vectorized Monte‑Carlo or fast tensor proxies to shortlist candidates, then apply exact HV only on the shortlist with cached/incremental updates and a small swap-based local search. Vectorize dominance precomputation, avoid rebuilding objects in loops, and keep all heavy work batched.

Next generation heuristics should: (1) pareto‑filter then shortlist by cheap proxy; (2) compute exact marginal HVC only on that shortlist using cached/incremental HV data structures; (3) vectorize or Monte‑Carlo only when needed; (4) cap HV evaluations and local search adaptively; and (5) reuse pygmo objects and add deterministic seeding and parallel shortlist evaluation.
bad_reflections: Use a Pareto filter + strong box‑volume proxy, then CELF lazy greedy with cached marginal gains and timestamps to avoid re-evaluations. Limit candidate pool adaptively (O(max(k,200))), reuse/avoid rebuilding HV objects, cap Monte‑Carlo samples, and run first‑improvement bounded swaps on a proxy-ranked small pool. Vectorize dominance and distance calculations; profile HV calls and budget them deterministically.

Use pareto filtering + box‑volume proxy to cap candidates, apply CELF lazy greedy with cached marginal gains keyed by (candidate, selection_size), limit shortlist size (∼min(200,8·k)), reuse current HV to compute deltas, and finish with a tiny bounded swap search. Avoid full O(n·k) HV scans and repeated hypervolume-object recreation.

Prefer Pareto filter + tight proxy upper bounds + CELF lazy greedy with cached (timestamped) marginal gains, small O(k log n) candidate pools, reuse hypervolume objects or incremental HV updates, vectorize dominance filtering, and cap local search. This minimizes HV calls while keeping HV quality high.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto-filter; proxy shortlisting; CELF lazy greedy; cached/incremental marginal gains; HV caps; bounded swap search; vectorization; incremental HV; deterministic seeding; parallel HV.
- Advice: Pareto-filter then form a tiny proxy-ranked shortlist O(max(k,200)); run CELF-style lazy greedy with cached/incremental marginals and strict HV-call caps; vectorize dominance/proxy work; parallelize HV evals; finish with a bounded first‑improvement swap.
- Avoid: full O(n·k) HV scans, rebuilding HV objects in loops, unbounded Monte‑Carlo sampling, non-deterministic seeding, and unbatched heavy work.
- Explanation: Cuts expensive HV calls, yields predictable low runtime and high hypervolume via tight proxies, caching, and lightweight local search.
